meta.title: Demonstrate understanding of macro-economic influences on the New Zealand economy
meta.purpose: This achievement standard involves demonstrating understanding of macro-economic influences on the New Zealand economy.

grade_descriptors.N1: Evidence does not reach the minimum threshold for Achieved-level indicators.
grade_descriptors.N2: Some Achieved-level indicators are met, but not enough to satisfy all required Achieved criteria.
grade_descriptors.A3: All Achieved-level indicators are met; no Merit-level indicator meets the Merit threshold.
grade_descriptors.A4: All Achieved indicators are met and at least one Merit indicator is met, but overall performance is below the Merit threshold.
grade_descriptors.M5: All Achieved indicators are met and the Merit threshold is reached; no Excellence-level indicator meets the Excellence threshold.
grade_descriptors.M6: All Achieved indicators are met, all Merit indicators are met, and at least one Excellence indicator is met, but overall performance is below the Excellence threshold.
grade_descriptors.E7: All Achieved and Merit indicators are met and the Excellence threshold is reached, but not all Excellence indicators are met.
grade_descriptors.E8: All Achieved, Merit, and Excellence indicators are met.

key_terms.definition: internal (e.g. consumption, investment, government policy) and external (e.g. exchange rates, world economy) factors affecting the economy
government goals such as price stability, economic growth, full employment, and balanced current account
graphical or conceptual representations such as the AD/AS model or circular flow model used to explain economic concepts

omis.description: Defines macro-economic influences including both internal and external factors

positive_signals: Names at least one internal macro-economic factor
Names at least one external macro-economic factor
Uses examples such as consumption, investment, exchange rates, or the world economy
negative_signals: Lists only internal factors or only external factors
Uses vague terms without identifying macro-economic influences
Provides a one-sided definition with no contrast between internal and external
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Students often define macro-economic influences with only one side of the economy, but Achieved requires both internal and external factors.

success_examples: Changes in government policy or international trade conditions can affect economic outcomes.

omis.description: Uses economic model(s) to illustrate concepts relating to macro-economic influences

positive_signals: Includes a labelled macro-economic model or graph
Links the model to a relevant macro-economic concept
Explains what the model shows about the influence being discussed
negative_signals: Uses an unlabelled graph or model
Includes a model with no link to the macro concept
Uses an irrelevant diagram unrelated to the explanation
evidence_guide.common_confusion: A diagram on its own is not enough; the model must be labelled and used to explain the macro-economic idea.

success_examples: An increase in government spending shifts the AD curve rightward, raising output and employment.

omis.description: Describes current state of the NZ economy using valid indicators

positive_signals: Uses a valid macro-economic indicator such as unemployment, inflation, or GDP growth
Interprets the indicator in a New Zealand context
Explains what the indicator suggests about current economic conditions
negative_signals: Lists an indicator with no interpretation
Describes the economy without any valid indicator
Uses a statistic with no contextual explanation
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Naming an indicator is insufficient unless the student also interprets what it means for the New Zealand economy.

success_examples: The current unemployment rate is 4.3%, showing near full employment.

omis.description: Correctly calculates or interprets macro-economic values

positive_signals: Shows the correct working for a macro-economic calculation
Explains how a rate or value has been interpreted
Uses correct macro-economic terminology when discussing the value
negative_signals: States a final value without showing working or explanation
Uses incorrect working or misinterprets the result
Gives a macro-economic value with no explanation of what it represents
evidence_guide.common_confusion: A correct-looking answer is not enough if the working or interpretation is missing.

omis.description: Explains how a macro-economic influence affects the NZ economy

positive_signals: Links a specific macro-economic influence to an economic effect
Uses cause-and-effect language such as 'because', 'therefore', or 'leads to'
Explains how the influence changes an outcome such as exports, inflation, or employment
negative_signals: Describes an influence without explaining the resulting effect
Uses generic statements such as 'it affects the economy' with no mechanism
Mentions an effect without connecting it back to the macro-economic influence
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Students often describe the influence and the outcome separately, but Merit requires an explicit causal link between them.

success_examples: A stronger NZ dollar makes exports more expensive, reducing demand.

omis.description: Links multiple graphs or models to support explanation

positive_signals: Refers to more than one graph or model
Explains how the models work together in the explanation
Connects the diagrams to the written macro-economic narrative
negative_signals: Refers to only one graph or model
Mentions multiple diagrams without linking them to the explanation
Uses models as decoration rather than explanatory support
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Simply including two diagrams is insufficient unless the student explicitly links them to the explanation.

success_examples: The circular flow shows income increase, which shifts the AD curve rightward in the AD/AS model.

omis.description: Refers to and integrates relevant resource material in explanations

positive_signals: Quotes or paraphrases information from the provided resource material
Uses stimulus data or statements to support the explanation
Integrates the resource material into the macro-economic argument
negative_signals: Gives a generic response with no reference to the supplied material
Mentions the resource without using its information
Uses unrelated evidence instead of the provided stimulus
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Students often know the content generally, but this indicator requires them to use the supplied resource material directly.

success_examples: The article notes investment rose by 3.2%, which contributed to GDP growth.

omis.description: Compares effectiveness of different government policies on one macro-economic goal

positive_signals: Evaluates at least two government policies
Compares how well each policy meets the same macro-economic goal
Uses criteria or reasoning to judge relative effectiveness
negative_signals: Describes only one policy
Lists policies without evaluating how well they achieve the goal
Compares policies without tying them to a macro-economic goal
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Excellence requires judgment about effectiveness, not just description of multiple policies.

success_examples: Fiscal policy has a delayed effect on inflation, while monetary policy can act more swiftly.

omis.description: Contrasts the impact of macro-economic influences on different goals

positive_signals: Shows contrasting impacts on different macro-economic goals
Uses trade-off language such as 'but', 'however', or 'while'
Explains how one influence can help one goal while harming another
negative_signals: Describes only one impact or one goal
Lists multiple impacts without contrasting them
Mentions trade-offs without explaining the different macro-economic goals involved
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Students often mention several effects, but this indicator requires them to contrast how the influence affects different goals.

success_examples: Increased exports support GDP growth but may raise inflationary pressure.

omis.description: Integrates economic models into comparisons of macro-economic impacts

positive_signals: Uses economic models inside the compare-or-contrast explanation
Explains how the models support the contrast in impacts
Treats the models as part of the argument rather than stand-alone visuals
negative_signals: Adds models as separate diagrams without integrating them into the comparison
Mentions models with no narrative explanation
Compares impacts without any model-based support
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Referring to a model is not enough unless it is embedded in the compare-and-contrast reasoning.

success_examples: The AD/AS model shows how both tax cuts and subsidies shift the AD curve rightward.
